nativeRange.habitat: The Starry Puffer inhabits marine and brackish reef-associated waters at depths of roughly 3 to 58 m. Juveniles tend to occupy sandy, weedy inner reefs and estuaries, while adults are more commonly encountered on deeper outer reef slopes.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on a variety of meaty foods in captivity, including squid, krill, clams, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey is generally advised because the species' beak-like fused teeth grow continuously and benefit from regular wear. In the wild, the Starry Puffer is considered omnivorous at a moderate trophic level (around 3.7), feeding primarily on benthic invertebrates and some algae.

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon, 1200x887. Sharp, well-lit complete lateral view of a live individual on a sandy reef; pale cream body densely covered in dark 'starry' spots incl. spotted fins - textbook diagnostic look.
portioid, 2048x1176. Crisp full lateral profile of an adult; dense black spotting over whitish body extending onto belly and caudal fin. Different individual/angle for variety.
Diego Delso, 5783x3855. High-res sharp full-body adult resting by reef in the Red Sea; pale body with dense black spots clearly shown. Bright daylight, complete animal.

summary: An Indo-Pacific giant puffer that has been reported to exceed a metre in length; generally considered semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a very large fish-only system with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Arothron stellatus is widely reported as one of the largest pufferfishes, with wild adults occasionally reaching around 120 cm; captive specimens more commonly grow to 50–90 cm but can still attain very large sizes given time and space. The species ranges across the Indo-Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa eastward to the Tuamotus, northward to southern Japan and southward to Lord Howe Island, and is generally encountered on reef slopes, patch reefs, and adjacent sandy areas.

Juveniles and adults differ markedly: juveniles tend to show dark belly stripes that gradually break up into the dense black spotting characteristic of mature fish. Because juveniles are sometimes sold under different trade descriptions than adults, buyers are advised to anticipate substantial growth.

Minimum tank size is a subject of meaningful disagreement among sources: hobby-oriented retailers suggest anywhere from roughly 750 L (200 gal) to 1,360 L (360 gal), while care guides for fully grown adults generally recommend going substantially larger. Given the species' eventual size, an enclosure on the order of 1,500 L (about 400 gal) or larger is generally advised for long-term housing, with plenty of open swimming space and rockwork providing caves and crevices.

Like other members of Tetraodontidae, A. stellatus is capable of inflating its body by ingesting water when threatened; it should not be allowed to gulp air, as this can be fatal. Transfers are generally recommended via a container rather than a net to reduce stress and prevent inflation.

The species' flesh and organs contain tetrodotoxin and it is poisonous to eat; this is a human-safety concern rather than a tank-water toxicity hazard in the same sense as boxfishes. Heavy filtration is generally advised given the species' messy feeding habits. Reproduction has rarely if ever been documented in home aquaria.
breedingNotes: The Starry Puffer has not been reliably bred in home aquaria. The species is egg-laying (oviparous) in the wild, but no captive breeding protocols have been documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page used for core husbandry parameters (temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max size 30 in, min tank 300 gal, semi-aggressive, not reef safe).

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 and could not be fetched directly; swapped in alternative secondaries (FishBase, Reeflings, Quality Marine) for cross-verification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), max length (120 cm TL, common 54 cm), temperature range (22.4–29°C), depth (3–58 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, and juvenile-to-adult coloration shift (dark belly stripes becoming spots).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to 120 cm), minimum tank (750 L / 200 gal), temperature (24–27°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, semi-aggressive/territorial temperament, and not reef safe.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (~48 in / ~120 cm), minimum tank (360 gal / 1363 L), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), care level (moderate), and not reef safe. Tank-size spread across sources is 750–1360 L; final minVolumeLiters biased to 1500 L per playbook tank-size rule given the species' eventual size.

fish.breedingNotes: Not reliably achieved in home aquaria. Oviparous in the wild.
